title_slide: 7 Simple Habits That Quietly Rewired My Productivity

slides.heading: Why Productivity Systems Fail
bullet_points: They require a perfect structure across an imperfect life
Complexity collapses on hard days
The fix: habits simple enough to survive your worst day
slides.speaker_note: Frame the problem before the solution — audiences need to feel seen before they'll listen.

slides.heading: Habit 1: The Shutdown Ritual
bullet_points: Four minutes: scan tasks, note what's open, write one line on what got done
Say out loud: 'Shutdown complete'
Gives your nervous system a real closure signal — brains fixate on open loops until you do
slides.speaker_note: Zeigarnik effect — unfinished tasks occupy working memory until a deliberate closure is registered.

slides.heading: Habit 2: The Weekly Brain Dump
bullet_points: 20 minutes, once a week — write everything in your head
No organization, no categories, just out
Offloading frees cognitive resources for actual thinking
slides.speaker_note: Emphasize: the value is in the emptying, not the filing — don't try to organize it.

slides.heading: Habit 3: Eat One Frog
bullet_points: Identify your most dreaded task the night before
Do it first — and only one
Avoidance runs in the background like an open program; one frog closes it
slides.speaker_note: The 'only one' constraint is what most people miss — more frogs multiplies drag, not output.

slides.heading: Habit 4: The Done List
bullet_points: At day's end, write the 5 most meaningful things you did
Not everything — what moved something
Corrects your estimate of your own capacity over time
slides.speaker_note: We chronically underestimate daily output — this is a calibration tool, not a self-congratulation ritual.

slides.heading: Habit 5: One 90-Minute Deep Work Block
bullet_points: First 15–20 minutes are on-ramp; short blocks end right as you hit stride
Each context switch costs 15–20 minutes of recovery time
One protected block beats four fragmented Pomodoros for output quality
slides.speaker_note: Csikszentmihalyi's flow research backs this — the on-ramp is real and short blocks consistently kill it.

slides.heading: Habit 6: Write Tomorrow's Top 3 Tonight
bullet_points: Three priorities, written the night before — three forces a choice, five doesn't
Night-before planning uses full context; morning planning uses depleted decision capacity
Morning-you should never decide what to work on — past-you already did
slides.speaker_note: Decision fatigue starts at zero each morning — the night-before plan sidesteps the worst decision window of the day.

slides.heading: Habit 7: Track One Metric Per Week
bullet_points: One number — whatever matters most this week
Tracking ten things creates measurement overhead that becomes its own procrastination
What gets measured gets noticed. Noticing is most of the work.
slides.speaker_note: Close with the pattern: all seven create moments of honest contact with how you're spending attention.
